meta.title: SANAD Breakthrough-Seizure Outcomes
meta.authors: Bonnett LJ, Powell GA, Tudur Smith C, et al.
meta.outcome: Breakthrough seizure, subsequent seizure recurrence, and re-remission after a period of 12-month remission (SANAD)
meta.clinical_utility: Three group-average outcomes after a first 12-month remission on treatment (SANAD; Bonnett 2017): whether a breakthrough seizure returns, whether more seizures follow a breakthrough, and the chance of regaining a 12-month remission. Not a personal risk score.
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. Three group-average outcomes for people with newly-diagnosed epilepsy who first became seizure-free for 12 months on medication (SANAD trial; Bonnett et al., PLOS ONE 2017). It is not a personal risk score — each is an overall curve.

Key findings

  • A first breakthrough seizure is common over time — about 1 in 4 within a year, and ~55% by 5–6 years.
  • Once a breakthrough happens, a further seizure is likely — ~74% within 2 years (often within a month).
  • But most people regain control — ~64% re-achieve a 12-month remission within 2 years.

Use the outcome selector to switch curves and the time slider to read any month. Clinical factors (neurological insult, more prior tonic-clonic seizures, a longer time to first remission) raised breakthrough risk in the study, but came from separate models and are not combined here.

model.selector_info:
Three group-average outcomes after a first 12-month remission on treatment — pick which curve to show:
• First breakthrough seizure — starting from 12 months seizure-free, will a seizure return? (Common over time.)
• Further seizure after a breakthrough — applies ONLY once a breakthrough has happened; the clock re-starts there. If you break through, another seizure is likely (~74% within 2 years).
• Regaining a 12-month remission — for the same people, the chance of getting back to a full year seizure-free (~64% by 2 years).
Each is an overall curve — there is no personal score.

breakthrough.info: Starting from 12 months seizure-free — will a seizure return? ~1 in 4 by 1 year, ~55% by 5–6 years.

recurrence.info: Applies only after a breakthrough (clock re-starts): another seizure is likely — ~74% within 2 years.

remission.info: The hopeful view for the same people: ~64% regain a full year seizure-free within 2 years.

provenance.source_paper: PMID28698335_2017_Risk of a seizure recurrence after a breakthrough seizu.pdf
provenance.digitizer: riskcalc auto-trace (contiguous-run axis detection + topmost-dark trace)
provenance.notes: Values digitised from the paper's own Kaplan–Meier curves (Figs 2–4); the paper reports no score/points. Curve ceilings from the digitised traces: breakthrough-seizure risk rises to ~58% by ~5.6yr (24% at 1yr, 37% at 2yr); seizure-recurrence-after-breakthrough rises to a ~76% ceiling (68% at 1yr, 74% at 2yr); chance of re-achieving 12-month remission is flat until 12mo then rises to ~82% (29% at 1yr, 64% at 2yr). Separately, the paper's TEXT quotes flow-through fractions of the SANAD cohort (34% proceed to a first breakthrough, 63% of those go on to recurrence, 44% re-achieve remission) — these are cohort proportions, NOT the curve ceilings. Human review of the three overlays required before deploy. | 2026-07-07 human-reviewed: digitized curves verified against Bonnett 2017 Figs 2-4 (breakthrough 37%@2yr, recurrence 74%@2yr, remission 64%@2yr).
